questions.question: What is a service-oriented life according to this course?

options.text: One dramatic act of service
An ongoing pattern of values-aligned choices integrated into life—a way of being, built on habits and sustainability, not heroics
Sacrificing all personal needs for others
Volunteering 40+ hours per week

questions.explanation: Service-oriented living is: ongoing pattern of choices (not one big act), integrated into existing roles (not bolted on), sustainable through habits and boundaries (not heroic sacrifice), values-driven (not guilt-driven), and reflective/adaptive (improves over time). It's a way of being that weaves contribution through daily life while maintaining personal wellbeing.

questions.question: Why does the course emphasize starting with WHY (values/meaning) before WHAT (specific activity)?

options.text: WHY doesn't matter; just start doing something
Your values-based motivation sustains when circumstances change and motivation wanes—activities shift, but purpose endures
To make the plan more complicated
Activities are more important than values

questions.explanation: Starting with WHY (values, meaning, purpose) creates durable motivation. When you're tired, when obstacles arise, when initial enthusiasm fades—your WHY sustains you. Activities may change (you might switch organizations, roles, or focus areas), but deeply held values endure. 'I serve because reducing elder isolation matters to me deeply' outlasts 'I serve because it seems like a good thing to do.' Connect service to personal meaning.

questions.question: What is the recommended starting commitment size and why?

options.text: As much as possible to maximize impact
Start ridiculously small (25% of what you think you can do)—consistency beats intensity; build habits, expand later
Wait until you have 20+ hours/week available
Commit to whatever feels good in the moment

questions.explanation: Start with 25% of what you think you can sustain. Why? The sustainability paradox: people who overcommit often burn out and quit entirely; those who start small build durable habits. Even 1-2 hours/month creates cumulative impact over years. You can always expand after 3-6 months of consistency. But if you start too big and burn out, you may never return. Small, sustainable beats large, unsustainable.

questions.question: Why are boundaries essential in a service plan, not optional?

options.text: Boundaries are selfish and prevent authentic service
Clear boundaries protect capacity, prevent caregiver burden (63% rate), enable decades of contribution, and maintain service quality
Good servants have no boundaries
Boundaries are only for professionals

questions.explanation: Boundaries are essential infrastructure for sustainable service: they protect your capacity (prevent the U-shaped curve harm), prevent caregiver burden (remember 63% severe burden rate from Lesson 2), enable decades rather than months of contribution, and maintain quality (exhausted servants give poor service). Clear nos ('I won't take calls outside scheduled time,' 'I won't serve when depleted') protect your yeses. Boundaries and compassion coexist.

questions.question: What should you do this week to implement your service plan?

options.text: Wait for perfect conditions before starting
Take one small action: make a phone call, schedule first service time, tell someone your commitment, or complete one 15-minute micro-action
Spend months researching before acting
Nothing—plans don't require action

questions.explanation: Implementation beats ideation. This week, take ONE small action: contact a potential organization, schedule your first service time in your calendar, tell one person about your commitment, or complete one 15-minute action that serves your community. Small actions build momentum. Start before you feel ready. Waiting for perfect conditions means never starting. Progress requires movement, however small. What will you do this week?
